candidate_problems.text: How can 'load-bearing' functional invariants be identified and measured independently of our descriptive choices? The conjecture depends on a real distinction between abstract constraint architecture and interchangeable biochemical detail, but it leaves open a criterion for when a motif, module, or map is genuinely causally/explanatorily indispensable rather than an observer-imposed abstraction. A strong research problem is to develop intervention-based or comparative tests that reveal which organizational features are truly invariant across realizations and which are merely convenient redescriptions.

Under what conditions does robustness actually promote evolvability rather than trap systems in locally stable but innovation-poor regimes? The conjecture claims neutral neighborhoods and modular insulation enlarge accessible search, but robustness can also canalize development, suppress phenotypic variation, and reduce selectable novelty. The unresolved tension is to specify the structural boundary conditions—environmental variability, population size, genotype-phenotype map geometry, modular coupling, timescale separation—under which robustness shifts from enabling adaptation to impeding it.

What generative evolutionary processes produce sparse, composable, insulated constraint architectures in the first place? The conjecture explains why such architectures would reconcile robustness and evolvability, but not how unguided evolution reliably discovers and maintains them despite historical contingency, path dependence, and selection acting on immediate fitness rather than abstract organization. This opens the problem of deriving whether feedback, redundancy, degeneracy, and hierarchy emerge as attractors of evolution, and if so through what mechanisms and tradeoffs.
